questions.question_text: Under AS 34.70, when must a seller deliver the Residential Real Property Transfer Disclosure Statement to the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Before the buyer makes a written offer
distractors: Within 10 days of accepting the offer
At the closing table before signing
Within 5 days of listing the property
questions.explanation: Alaska law requires the seller to deliver the transfer disclosure statement before the buyer makes a written offer. This ensures buyers have information about the property's condition before committing to a purchase.

questions.question_text: A seller completes the Alaska transfer disclosure in good faith but unknowingly omits a hidden plumbing defect. Is the seller liable?
questions.correct_answer: Not for unknown defects disclosed in good faith
distractors: Yes, strict liability applies to all defects
Yes, the seller guarantees property condition
Only if the defect is discovered within one year
questions.explanation: The disclosure must be made in good faith, covering known defects and conditions. A seller is not liable for defects they genuinely did not know about if they completed the disclosure honestly. The requirement is to disclose what is known, not to guarantee condition.

questions.question_text: What must the Alaska Residential Real Property Transfer Disclosure Statement cover?
questions.correct_answer: Known defects and conditions of the property
distractors: Only structural defects found by an inspector
Only defects that cost more than $500 to repair
All defects regardless of seller's knowledge
questions.explanation: The disclosure covers known defects and conditions of the property as disclosed by the seller in good faith. It is not limited to structural issues or those above a dollar threshold, nor does it require the seller to discover unknown defects.

questions.question_text: A seller discovers a new roof leak after delivering the transfer disclosure to the buyer. What must the seller do?
questions.correct_answer: Deliver an amendment to the disclosure to the buyer
distractors: Nothing, the original disclosure is final
Cancel the listing and relist the property
Repair the leak before closing without disclosing
questions.explanation: If information in the disclosure becomes inaccurate after delivery, the seller must deliver an amendment to the buyer. The obligation to disclose continues throughout the transaction — new material defects must be communicated.

questions.question_text: A listing agent knows the seller's transfer disclosure omits a known basement flooding issue. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Advise the seller to amend the disclosure
distractors: Ignore it since it is the seller's responsibility
Correct the disclosure without the seller's knowledge
Report the seller to AREC immediately
questions.explanation: Licensees have a duty to ensure material facts are disclosed. The agent should advise the seller to amend the disclosure to include the basement flooding. If the seller refuses, the agent may have additional obligations including disclosing the material fact directly.

questions.question_text: A seller delivers the transfer disclosure after the buyer has already made an offer. The disclosure is hand-delivered. How long does the buyer have to terminate?
distractors: 5 days
7 days
10 days
questions.explanation: If the transfer disclosure is delivered after the buyer makes an offer and is delivered in person, the buyer has 3 days to terminate the agreement. If mailed instead of hand-delivered, the rescission period extends to 6 days.

questions.question_text: A seller mails the late transfer disclosure to the buyer on Monday. How many days does the buyer have to cancel?
distractors: 3 days from receipt
10 days from mailing
5 days from receipt
questions.explanation: When the transfer disclosure is mailed rather than delivered in person, the buyer has 6 days to terminate. The longer period accounts for mail delivery time. If delivered in person, the rescission period is only 3 days.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives an amended disclosure revealing new foundation issues after the original was timely delivered. Does the buyer have rescission rights?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the same rescission periods apply to amendments
distractors: No, rescission applies only to the original disclosure
Yes, but only if the buyer hasn't waived inspection
No, the buyer must negotiate repairs instead
questions.explanation: When the seller delivers an amendment to the disclosure, the buyer's rescission rights are triggered again with the same time periods (3 days in person, 6 days mailed). Amendments revealing material changes give the buyer another opportunity to evaluate.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives the transfer disclosure before making an offer, as required by law. Does the buyer still have a right to cancel based on the disclosure?
questions.correct_answer: No, the rescission right applies only to late disclosures
distractors: Yes, the buyer has 3 days regardless of timing
Yes, the buyer has 15 days to review
No, unless the seller agrees to re-deliver the disclosure at closing
questions.explanation: The 3-day/6-day rescission rights only apply when the disclosure is delivered after the buyer makes an offer (late disclosure). When the disclosure is timely — delivered before the buyer's offer — the buyer uses the information to decide whether to make an offer at all.

questions.question_text: Which of the following transactions is exempt from Alaska's transfer disclosure requirement?
questions.correct_answer: First transfer of new construction never occupied
distractors: Sale of a property built before 1980
Transfer between non-related parties
Sale of any single-family residence
questions.explanation: The first transfer of new construction that has never been occupied is exempt from the transfer disclosure requirement under AS 34.70.120. The only other way around the requirement is a written waiver agreed to by both buyer and seller under AS 34.70.110 — Alaska does not exempt court-ordered transfers or foreclosures the way many states do.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller in Alaska agree in writing to waive the transfer disclosure requirement. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the parties may waive by written agreement
distractors: No, the disclosure is mandatory and cannot be waived
Yes, but only with AREC approval
Yes, but only for commercial properties
questions.explanation: Alaska is one of the states where the buyer and seller may mutually waive the transfer disclosure requirement by written agreement. This is an unusual provision — many states do not allow such waivers for residential properties.

questions.question_text: A seller willfully conceals a known defect on the transfer disclosure. What is the maximum penalty?
questions.correct_answer: Up to 3 times actual damages (treble damages)
distractors: Actual damages only plus court costs
A $10,000 fine from AREC
Criminal prosecution for fraud
questions.explanation: Willful violation of the transfer disclosure requirement exposes the seller to treble damages — up to 3 times the actual damages suffered. Negligent violations result in liability for actual damages only. This creates a strong incentive for honest disclosure.

questions.question_text: A seller negligently omits termite damage that was noted in an old inspection report the seller had forgotten about. What damages may the buyer recover?
distractors: Up to 3 times actual damages
A minimum of $5,000 statutory damages
No damages for negligent violations
questions.explanation: Negligent violation of the disclosure requirement results in liability for actual damages. Treble damages (up to 3x) are reserved for willful violations. The distinction between negligent and willful violation significantly affects the seller's exposure.

questions.question_text: Despite a reasonable effort, a seller cannot determine the answer to an item on the Alaska transfer disclosure form. What may the seller do?
questions.correct_answer: Provide a reasonable approximation, clearly labeled as an approximation
distractors: Leave the item blank without comment
Omit the disclosure statement entirely
Have the buyer complete that item
questions.explanation: Under AS 34.70.040(b), if information required by the disclosure form is unknown or unavailable despite a reasonable effort to ascertain it, the seller may use an approximation, provided it is clearly identified as an approximation, is reasonable, and is not used to circumvent the disclosure requirement.

questions.question_text: A seller hand-delivers a late disclosure on Wednesday. The buyer decides to terminate on Friday. Is this within the rescission period?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, Friday is within the 3-day period
distractors: No, the buyer only had until Thursday
No, the period starts the next business day
Yes, but only if the seller consents to the termination
questions.explanation: The 3-day rescission period for in-person delivery gives the buyer until Saturday (day 3) to terminate. Friday (day 2) is well within the allowed period. The right exists because the disclosure was late — delivered after the buyer made an offer — and is exercised by delivering written notice of termination to the seller or the seller's agent.
